items.q: What are the main Christian theories of atonement?
items.correct: Penal substitution (Christ bore the punishment for sin), Christus Victor (Christ defeated evil powers through the cross), ransom theory (Christ paid a price to free humanity), and moral exemplar (Christ's death inspires love). Different traditions emphasise different theories.
items.misconception: All Christians believe Jesus died only to pay God back for sin.
items.random_wrong: Atonement means Jesus apologised to humans for being born divine.
items.why: Penal substitution dominates Protestant evangelicalism but isn't the only or oldest model. Christus Victor was prevalent in the early church and Orthodox traditions still emphasise it. Students often treat one model as 'the' Christian view of atonement.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What distinction matters for the free will defence to the problem of evil?
items.correct: Between moral evil (caused by free human choices) and natural evil (earthquakes, disease) — the free will defence directly addresses moral evil but struggles with natural evil, which isn't a product of free choice.
items.misconception: The free will defence solves all forms of evil at once.
items.random_wrong: Natural evil means evil that is good for nature.
items.why: Students apply the free will defence to all evil. The challenge of natural evil is precisely that no one chose it, requiring other theodicies (Augustinian fallen world, Irenaean soul-making) to address it.

items.q: In which work does Anselm develop the satisfaction theory?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cur Deus Homo. “Proslogion” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Gustaf Aulen. “Karl Barth” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Anselm's Cur Deus Homo completed?
items.why: The real figure is 1098. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
